  "practiceType": "Concept Builder",
  "sectionNumber": "1.1",
  "outputLanguage": "en",
  "sectionOcrText": "# Chapter 1 – The Living World\n\n# 1.1 Diversity in the Living World (Deep OCR)\n\n---\n\n## Introduction\n\nHow wonderful is the living world! The wide range of living types is amazing. The extraordinary habitats in which we find living organisms, be it cold mountains, deciduous forests, oceans, fresh water lakes, deserts or hot springs, leave us speechless. The beauty of a galloping horse, of the migrating birds, the valley of flowers or the attacking shark evokes awe and a deep sense of wonder.\n\nThe ecological conflict and cooperation among members of a population and among populations of a community or even the molecular traffic inside a cell make us deeply reflect on—**what indeed is life?**\n\nThis question has two implicit questions within it.\n\nThe first is a technical one and seeks answer to **what living is as opposed to the non-living**, and the second is a philosophical one, and seeks answer to **what the purpose of life is**.\n\nAs scientists, we shall not attempt answering the second question. We will try to reflect on—**what is living?** \n\n---\n\n## Diversity Around Us\n\nIf you look around you will see a large variety of living organisms, be it potted plants, insects, birds, your pets or other animals and plants.\n\nThere are also several organisms that you cannot see with your naked eye but they are all around you.\n\nIf you were to increase the area that you make observations in, the range and variety of organisms that you see would increase. Obviously, if you were to visit a dense forest, you would probably see a much greater number and kinds of living organisms in it.\n\nEach different kind of plant, animal or organism that you see represents a **species**.\n\nThe number of species that are known and described ranges between **1.7–1.8 million**.\n\nThis refers to **biodiversity**, or the number and types of organisms present on Earth.\n\nWe should remember here that as we explore new areas, and even old ones, new organisms are continuously being identified. \n\n---\n\n## Need for Standardised Naming\n\nAs stated earlier, there are millions of plants and animals in the world; we know the plants and animals in our own area by their local names.\n\nThese local names would vary from place to place, even within a country.\n\nProbably you would recognise the confusion that would be created if we did not find ways and means to talk to each other, to refer to organisms we are talking about.\n\nHence, there is a need to standardise the naming of living organisms such that a particular organism is known by the same name all over the world.\n\nThis process is called **nomenclature**.\n\nObviously, nomenclature or naming is only possible when the organism is described correctly and we know to what organism the name is attached to.\n\nThis is **identification**. \n\n---\n\n## International Codes for Scientific Naming\n\nIn order to facilitate the study, a number of scientists have established procedures to assign a scientific name to each known organism. This is acceptable to biologists all over the world.\n\nFor plants, scientific names are based on agreed principles and criteria provided in the **International Code for Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN).**\n\nAnimal taxonomists have evolved the **International Code of Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN).**\n\nThe scientific names ensure that each organism has only one name.\n\nDescription of any organism should enable people (in any part of the world) to arrive at the same name.\n\nThey also ensure that such a name has not been used for any other known organism. \n\n---\n\n## Binomial Nomenclature\n\nBiologists follow universally accepted principles to provide scientific names to known organisms.\n\nEach name has two components:\n\n* Generic name\n* Specific epithet\n\nThis system of providing a name with two components is called **Binomial Nomenclature**.\n\nThis naming system given by **Carolus Linnaeus** is being practised by biologists all over the world.\n\nThis naming system using a two-word format was found convenient.\n\nLet us take the example of mango to understand the way of providing scientific names better.\n\nThe scientific name of mango is written as:\n\n*Mangifera indica*\n\nHere,\n\n* **Mangifera** represents the genus.\n* **indica** represents the species or specific epithet. \n\n---\n\n## Universal Rules of Nomenclature\n\n1. Biological names are generally in Latin and written in italics. They are Latinised or derived from Latin irrespective of their origin.\n\n2. The first word in a biological name represents the genus while the second component denotes the specific epithet.\n\n3. Both the words in a biological name, when handwritten, are separately underlined, or printed in italics to indicate their Latin origin.\n\n4. The first word denoting the genus starts with a capital letter while the specific epithet starts with a small letter.\n\nExample:\n\n*Mangifera indica*\n\nThe name of the author appears after the specific epithet, i.e., at the end of the biological name and is written in an abbreviated form.\n\nExample:\n\n*Mangifera indica* Linn.\n\nIt indicates that this species was first described by **Linnaeus**. \n\n---\n\n## Classification\n\nSince it is nearly impossible to study all the living organisms, it is necessary to devise some means to make this possible.\n\nThis process is **classification**.\n\nClassification is the process by which anything is grouped into convenient categories based on some easily observable characters.\n\nFor example, we easily recognise groups such as:\n\n* Plants\n* Animals\n* Dogs\n* Cats\n* Insects\n\nThe moment we use any of these terms, we associate certain characters with the organisms in that group.\n\nSimilarly,\n\n* Mammals are recognised as animals with external ears and body hair.\n* Wheat immediately refers to wheat plants and not rice or any other plant.\n\nThus,\n\n* Dogs\n* Cats\n* Mammals\n* Wheat\n* Rice\n* Plants\n* Animals\n\nare convenient categories used to study organisms.\n\nThe scientific term for these categories is **taxa**.\n\nTaxa can indicate categories at very different levels.\n\nFor example,\n\n* Plants form a taxon.\n* Wheat is also a taxon.\n* Animals, mammals and dogs are all taxa, but at different hierarchical levels.\n\nA dog is a mammal, and mammals are animals.\n\nTherefore, **animals, mammals and dogs represent taxa at different levels.** \n\n---\n\n## Taxonomy\n\nBased on characteristics, all living organisms can be classified into different taxa.\n\nThis process of classification is called **taxonomy**.\n\nExternal and internal structure, along with the structure of the cell, developmental process and ecological information of organisms are essential and form the basis of modern taxonomic studies.\n\nHence,\n\n* Characterisation\n* Identification\n* Classification\n* Nomenclature\n\nare the processes that are basic to taxonomy. \n\n---\n\n## Early Classification\n\nTaxonomy is not something new.\n\nHuman beings have always been interested in knowing more and more about the various kinds of organisms, particularly with reference to their own use.\n\nIn early days, human beings needed to find sources for their basic needs of:\n\n* Food\n* Clothing\n* Shelter\n\nHence, the earliest classifications were based on the **uses** of various organisms. \n\n---\n\n## Systematics\n\nHuman beings were, since long, not only interested in knowing more about different kinds of organisms and their diversities, but also the relationships among them.\n\nThis branch of study was referred to as **systematics**.\n\nThe word **systematics** is derived from the Latin word **systema**, which means **systematic arrangement of organisms**.\n\nLinnaeus used **Systema Naturae** as the title of his publication.\n\nThe scope of systematics was later enlarged to include:\n\n* Identification\n* Nomenclature\n* Classification\n\nSystematics also takes into account **evolutionary relationships between organisms.** \n",

      "normalized_text": "# Chapter 1 – The Living World\n\n# 1.1 Diversity in the Living World (Deep OCR)\n\n---\n\n## Introduction\n\nHow wonderful is the living world! The wide range of living types is amazing. The extraordinary habitats in which we find living organisms, be it cold mountains, deciduous forests, oceans, fresh water lakes, deserts or hot springs, leave us speechless. The beauty of a galloping horse, of the migrating birds, the valley of flowers or the attacking shark evokes awe and a deep sense of wonder.\n\nThe ecological conflict and cooperation among members of a population and among populations of a community or even the molecular traffic inside a cell make us deeply reflect on—**what indeed is life?**\n\nThis question has two implicit questions within it.\n\nThe first is a technical one and seeks answer to **what living is as opposed to the non-living**, and the second is a philosophical one, and seeks answer to **what the purpose of life is**.\n\nAs scientists, we shall not attempt answering the second question. We will try to reflect on—**what is living?** \n\n---\n\n## Diversity Around Us\n\nIf you look around you will see a large variety of living organisms, be it potted plants, insects, birds, your pets or other animals and plants.\n\nThere are also several organisms that you cannot see with your naked eye but they are all around you.\n\nIf you were to increase the area that you make observations in, the range and variety of organisms that you see would increase. Obviously, if you were to visit a dense forest, you would probably see a much greater number and kinds of living organisms in it.\n\nEach different kind of plant, animal or organism that you see represents a **species**.\n\nThe number of species that are known and described ranges between **1.7–1.8 million**.\n\nThis refers to **biodiversity**, or the number and types of organisms present on Earth.\n\nWe should remember here that as we explore new areas, and even old ones, new organisms are continuously being identified. \n\n---\n\n## Need for Standardised Naming\n\nAs stated earlier, there are millions of plants and animals in the world; we know the plants and animals in our own area by their local names.\n\nThese local names would vary from place to place, even within a country.\n\nProbably you would recognise the confusion that would be created if we did not find ways and means to talk to each other, to refer to organisms we are talking about.\n\nHence, there is a need to standardise the naming of living organisms such that a particular organism is known by the same name all over the world.\n\nThis process is called **nomenclature**.\n\nObviously, nomenclature or naming is only possible when the organism is described correctly and we know to what organism the name is attached to.\n\nThis is **identification**. \n\n---\n\n## International Codes for Scientific Naming\n\nIn order to facilitate the study, a number of scientists have established procedures to assign a scientific name to each known organism. This is acceptable to biologists all over the world.\n\nFor plants, scientific names are based on agreed principles and criteria provided in the **International Code for Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN).**\n\nAnimal taxonomists have evolved the **International Code of Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN).**\n\nThe scientific names ensure that each organism has only one name.\n\nDescription of any organism should enable people (in any part of the world) to arrive at the same name.\n\nThey also ensure that such a name has not been used for any other known organism. \n\n---\n\n## Binomial Nomenclature\n\nBiologists follow universally accepted principles to provide scientific names to known organisms.\n\nEach name has two components:\n\n* Generic name\n* Specific epithet\n\nThis system of providing a name with two components is called **Binomial Nomenclature**.\n\nThis naming system given by **Carolus Linnaeus** is being practised by biologists all over the world.\n\nThis naming system using a two-word format was found convenient.\n\nLet us take the example of mango to understand the way of providing scientific names better.\n\nThe scientific name of mango is written as:\n\n*Mangifera indica*\n\nHere,\n\n* **Mangifera** represents the genus.\n* **indica** represents the species or specific epithet. \n\n---\n\n## Universal Rules of Nomenclature\n\n1. Biological names are generally in Latin and written in italics. They are Latinised or derived from Latin irrespective of their origin.\n\n2. The first word in a biological name represents the genus while the second component denotes the specific epithet.\n\n3. Both the words in a biological name, when handwritten, are separately underlined, or printed in italics to indicate their Latin origin.\n\n4. The first word denoting the genus starts with a capital letter while the specific epithet starts with a small letter.\n\nExample:\n\n*Mangifera indica*\n\nThe name of the author appears after the specific epithet, i.e., at the end of the biological name and is written in an abbreviated form.\n\nExample:\n\n*Mangifera indica* Linn.\n\nIt indicates that this species was first described by **Linnaeus**. \n\n---\n\n## Classification\n\nSince it is nearly impossible to study all the living organisms, it is necessary to devise some means to make this possible.\n\nThis process is **classification**.\n\nClassification is the process by which anything is grouped into convenient categories based on some easily observable characters.\n\nFor example, we easily recognise groups such as:\n\n* Plants\n* Animals\n* Dogs\n* Cats\n* Insects\n\nThe moment we use any of these terms, we associate certain characters with the organisms in that group.\n\nSimilarly,\n\n* Mammals are recognised as animals with external ears and body hair.\n* Wheat immediately refers to wheat plants and not rice or any other plant.\n\nThus,\n\n* Dogs\n* Cats\n* Mammals\n* Wheat\n* Rice\n* Plants\n* Animals\n\nare convenient categories used to study organisms.\n\nThe scientific term for these categories is **taxa**.\n\nTaxa can indicate categories at very different levels.\n\nFor example,\n\n* Plants form a taxon.\n* Wheat is also a taxon.\n* Animals, mammals and dogs are all taxa, but at different hierarchical levels.\n\nA dog is a mammal, and mammals are animals.\n\nTherefore, **animals, mammals and dogs represent taxa at different levels.** \n\n---\n\n## Taxonomy\n\nBased on characteristics, all living organisms can be classified into different taxa.\n\nThis process of classification is called **taxonomy**.\n\nExternal and internal structure, along with the structure of the cell, developmental process and ecological information of organisms are essential and form the basis of modern taxonomic studies.\n\nHence,\n\n* Characterisation\n* Identification\n* Classification\n* Nomenclature\n\nare the processes that are basic to taxonomy. \n\n---\n\n## Early Classification\n\nTaxonomy is not something new.\n\nHuman beings have always been interested in knowing more and more about the various kinds of organisms, particularly with reference to their own use.\n\nIn early days, human beings needed to find sources for their basic needs of:\n\n* Food\n* Clothing\n* Shelter\n\nHence, the earliest classifications were based on the **uses** of various organisms. \n\n---\n\n## Systematics\n\nHuman beings were, since long, not only interested in knowing more about different kinds of organisms and their diversities, but also the relationships among them.\n\nThis branch of study was referred to as **systematics**.\n\nThe word **systematics** is derived from the Latin word **systema**, which means **systematic arrangement of organisms**.\n\nLinnaeus used **Systema Naturae** as the title of his publication.\n\nThe scope of systematics was later enlarged to include:\n\n* Identification\n* Nomenclature\n* Classification\n\nSystematics also takes into account **evolutionary relationships between organisms.**",

His system stuck and is now used worldwide, ensuring that a lion is always Panthera leo, no matter the local language.",

Item 2 establishes the logical sequence: you must first describe features (characterisation) to identify an organism, then classify it, and finally name it. Item 3 isolates 'Photosynthesis' as unrelated—it is a biological process, not a taxonomy process.
